chapters.title: Having the right business model is essential to the growth of any organization
chapters.text:
At the beginning of a quarterly meeting with her staff, Eileen Sharp was completely unaware of what she was getting herself into. An ordinary meeting quickly devolved into a full-blown catastrophe when the group's facilitators failed to communicate effectively. Sue Meecham, her sales vice president, attempted to leave on the same day, and Vic, her business partner and co-founder, accused her of being the root of the company's problems. Although Swan Services was her largest investment, Sharp struggled to deal with the strain of pulling everything back together, as revenue and human resource levels plummeted under her watch. The assistance of a professional consultant helped her recognize several aspects of the organization to which she had previously been blind.

The Entrepreneurial Operating System is not a theory. It’s not full of abstract concepts. It’s not about one big idea. Rather, it’s a complete system full of simple, practical tools that has helped thousands of entrepreneurs worldwide get what they want from their businesses. ~ Gino Wickman

This summary examines the underlying causes of Eileen Sharp's difficulties, as well as the methods she used to overcome them. The example provided here demonstrates how it is feasible to save a failing business while also creating a solid foundation to simplify future endeavors. It will also introduce a correctional framework rendered as a business fable, and leaders, business owners, and aspiring managers will learn a great deal on how to make positive changes in their organizations. The story of Swan Services, to which many leaders and organizations could relate, is the focus of this summary.

If you really want to build a successful firm with great leadership, you may need to change your management team.

chapters.title: Responding to a corporate crisis requires team leaders to devise a practical and proven strategy
chapters.text:
Sales and revenues for Swan Services had plummeted due to a significant drop in business. Swan's sales force had failed to meet quarterly revenue targets three times in five quarters, existing clients were leaving, the cost of labor had increased, and despite spending more money on talent, a few key employees had recently left the organization without explanation, putting the company's steady growth on hold. Swan's once impressive corporate culture that had emphasized hard work and outstanding results was beginning to deteriorate.

This decline resulted in a great deal of conflict among the company's management, pushing Sharp to seek assistance from outside the organization. Her business partner introduced her to Alan Roth, who worked as a business implementer, and whose job was to assist individuals in getting more out of their enterprises. Roth introduced Sharp and her team to EOS (Entrepreneurial Operating System).

When you clarify your vision, you will make better judgments regarding people and strategies.

EOS is suitable for small to medium-sized firms, and it addresses some of the typical challenges that entrepreneurial leadership teams face. Getting familiar with these difficulties is essential because without knowing where you got it wrong, you’ll hardly get it right. To identify these difficulties, you need to watch out for these signs:

• When it seems like your company, not you, is controlling your life, you’ll lack the required control to pilot the affairs of your organization.
• You feel unable to align your people — whether they are customers, suppliers, partners, or employees.
• When there aren't enough earnings and revenue generated, you’ll struggle to implement new ideas and keep your employees satisfied.
• You have reached a growth ceiling and feel stuck or unsure how to move forward.

When you identify several of these problems, you know that you need a thorough overview of your business and team.

chapters.title: There are six business fundamentals that every organization must adopt to get back on track
chapters.text:
The EOS model brings together tried and true principles, structures, and ideas from a variety of business sources to form a coherent whole that is easy to understand. Business owners must address the six pillars to establish a scalable and successful company: Vision, People, Data, Issues, Process, and Traction. However, before proceeding with this business model, you need to understand that for the EOS to be effective, a founder or owner must be prepared to undertake four mindset adjustments:

• Create a genuine leadership team to guide the organization. You cannot make all the decisions and carry out all the tasks. Hire individuals who are more capable than you, delegate specific management tasks and responsibilities to them, and show a unified face to the rest of the business to ensure success.
• Accept that organizations expand in spurts and that you will inevitably reach limits at the individual, departmental, and organizational levels. You and your team must be able to simplify, delegate, forecast challenges, systemize, and establish the appropriate framework for your next development phase if you are to succeed.
• Run your company on a single operating system to ensure everyone is on the same page. Do this by bringing the team together and limiting external influence.
• Make a point of being open-minded. Be honest and transparent about your weaknesses and be eager to accept new ideas.

When you have successfully adopted these four mindsets, you can begin implementing the first thing on the EOS model — Vision.

You need to develop and communicate a strong vision through open-mindedness and the desire to make things right, as this will give your team or employees something to work with.

Develop a compelling vision for your organization and help people to see it. When you align everyone in the same direction, it creates a laser-sharp focus that propels you forward. In the next chapter, you’ll find out more about vision and how vision helps you design a better work environment.

chapters.title: Define your goals by asking yourself important questions related to your organization
chapters.text:
Once you formulate a vision, the next step is to make it clear and understandable by using the Vision/Traction Organizer (V/TO) — a tool for getting your vision out of your mind to make strategic planning easier. You can use the V/TO to crystalize your vision on paper. The V/TO is a collection of eight questions that identify where you're going and how you'll get there. They are:

What are your fundamental principles?

These are three to seven timeless business concepts. Your objective is to uncover and clearly express your organization's values so they may influence all your choices. Communicate the key principles, embed them in your recruiting, firing, and reward systems, and embody them every day.

What’s your core focus?

This unique mix of leadership, abilities, and interests makes your firm exceptional. Knowing your focus allows you to concentrate on people, things, and behaviors that match it.

Your position as the leader doesn’t exempt you from the organization’s problems. Look inwards and find ways to improve yourself through self-evaluation.

What’s your 10-year goal?

Define your organization's future in ten years. The leadership team must debate and develop a vision that inspires and unites the whole business.

How do you market your company?

Define your ideal client and how you will service them to focus and help you identify your most important target customers.

What is your three-year vision?

This concept helps you plan for a future that is close enough to be encouraging. Ask your leadership team to compose and share their three-year scenario. Decide on the vision you want to paint, including sales, profit, and other metrics.

What is your one-year plan?

Set three to seven main priorities to fulfill in a year. Less is more — focus on a few critical objectives and set a budget to ensure you have the resources.

What are your quarterly rocks?

Your quarterly rocks are the priorities you set for your organization every four months. They help you evaluate your current position and work with your team to know the way forward.

What are the issues facing you?

These are the main roadblocks to your ambitions. Ask your team to brainstorm possibilities, dangers, and impediments that can heavily affect your organization. This way, you can have people contribute more to the growth of the business.

Prepare to replay your vision many times before it completely sinks in. Encourage workers to express their opinions and concerns since it strengthens dedication to the mission. Your responsibility is to communicate the vision and the V/TO clearly and compellingly; those who do not support you will ultimately leave.

chapters.title: Employees who share your company's fundamental principles and flourish in your culture are the right people to work with
chapters.text:
People and Data are the next two components of the EOS business model. These two frameworks are the most effective at assisting you in assembling the ideal team with the proper financial and human resources.

It pays to have the right individuals in the right seats when building a strong team, and this entails two steps:

Finding the ideal individuals for your company

You may do this by using the People Analyzer, a report card tool for determining how well existing and prospective workers align with your company's values. The basic values of each firm are distinct. The People Analyzer helps you cut through the many layers of interviewing someone and determine whether they represent your company's values and traits. You may assess if applicants align with your company by interviewing them based on their fundamental beliefs and delving into their motivations, interests, and aspirations.

Without data, you'll be relying on subjective judgment and emotions to manage your firm.

Putting the right people in the right seats

Once you've found the right people, you must put them in the proper seats, which means that their duties and responsibilities must match their specific talents, capabilities, and interests. Use the Accountability Chart to design the best structure for your business. The Accountability Chart is an organizational chart that specifies your company's divisions and duties.

After assembling the ideal team, the next stage is to collect the Data required to bring your company to life. To be successful in the data component, you must have two things: scorecards and measurables for everyone in the business.

Scorecards are leading indications that provide leadership teams with an absolute feel of the company. They are platforms on which everyone in an organization is scored based on several parameters.

Measurables — a vast clientele, units, or widgets — offer perspectives about every organization’s key numbers that indicate its size.

Did you know? According to Forbes, almost 60% of executives reported feeling exhausted at the end of the workday, which is a clear indicator of burnout.

chapters.title: Foster a solution-oriented culture, centralize operations, and realize your goals for business success
chapters.text:
The fourth component of the EOS model covers all the issues you experience while executing your ideas with your team and consumers. Issues are impediments to your objectives and vision. Most entrepreneurs are so preoccupied with overcoming barriers that they don't take the time to investigate the underlying issues. An open, problem-solving atmosphere to identify, examine and handle concerns at all levels makes it easy to recognize your faults as an individual and a team.

The IDS (Identify, Discuss, Solve) method is an effective problem-solving tool, but many businesses neglect or undervalue this basic technology. The team must identify the challenges impacting the company, debate them professionally, and provide solutions.

After dealing with an organization's problems, the Process begins. A typical organization has several interconnected basic processes, and these fundamental mechanisms are what connect visions and facts. Every firm should identify and codify its method of doing things and then implement and enhance the technique regularly to allow them the choice to replicate, expand, sustain, or market its enterprises.

The last phase in the EOS tool is traction. It refers to the process of carrying out or making your visions a reality. Set 90-day priorities and design a meeting framework to get traction. To make your idea a reality, you must carry out your plans and maintain momentum. Instill discipline and responsibility via two crucial components: Meeting Pulse and Rocks.

Set short-term priorities (Rocks) that will get you closer to your objective. Link three to seven quarterly goals to your vision for the organization, leadership team, and workers.

The Meeting Pulse consists of quarterly and weekly meetings where the leadership team gathers to drive development and handle problems. Alan Roth was a key figure in the development of Swan's quarterly Meeting Pulse. He said they'd meet four times a year — three times for one-day Quarterly Sessions and once for a two-day Annual Planning Session — and that he'd help them conduct those sessions for as long as they needed.

Well-run meetings are critical for accountability, outcomes, and efficiency.

With these well-detailed tools that many firms seem to ignore, Alan Roth was able to assist Swan Services to get back on track using EOS. Sales significantly improved, and the team was eager to try out the new things they had learned.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Deciding to incorporate EOS into your organization can move your company to the level of success you have always desired. You will experience less frustration and greater success as a result, and the entire company will see your vision since it will be obvious. The right model will translate your plans into a visible strategy so that it is no longer just an idea. Every member of your organization will be on the same page in terms of who you are as a company, where your company is going, and how you intend to get there. The company will benefit from cohesion and will be able to do more with less effort.

There are three stages in documenting your Way. First, identify your core processes. Then break down what happens in each one and document it. Finally, compile the information into a single package for everyone in your company. ~ Gino Wickman

A good business model will help you become more effective in your execution, and you will be more disciplined and responsible as a result. You will complete important tasks that are critical to realizing your goal. The days of unachievable ambitions and lack of responsibility will come to an end.

The problem that Swan Services faced can happen to any organization. At times, the market can become unpredictable and unstable, making the future look bleak and an organization lose cohesion and focus. However, when the desire to make things work meets with a good business model like the EOS, things can quickly change for the overall good.

Try this

Create a relaxed atmosphere in your organization by encouraging your employees and team members with positive remarks. Celebrate little wins and create monthly awards to promote competition. Listen to people's opinions, and don't be too harsh with your criticisms.

Read it till the end?
